  • Hide All Code

Power and cost: persuasion experiment

Design and statistics follow Röttger et al.’s ai-distortion persuasion follow-up (Study 5); the model is refit here and validated against their committed results (Appendix A).

1. Model and assumptions

Statistical model (identical to Paul’s persuasion_regressions_full_analyses.R):

\[\text{stance\_shift} \;\sim\; \text{arm} \;+\; (1\mid\text{reader}) \;+\; (1\mid\text{proposition})\]

a Gaussian linear mixed model (glmmTMB), crossed random intercepts for reader and proposition (no random slopes, as preregistered). The outcome is the signed attitude change toward the proposition, \(\text{attitude}_\text{post}-\text{attitude}_\text{pre}\), on the 0–100 scale. A for summary shifts it by \(+\delta\), an against summary by \(-\delta\), a neutral summary by 0.

Inference (Paul’s): an average marginal effect (AME) is computed with marginaleffects::avg_comparisons (delta method, re.form = NA) and detected when its 95% Wald CI excludes 0 — exactly as in Röttger et al.’s Figure 5.

Two experimental designs, both 6 paragraphs per participant, within-participant:

design arms per arm contrast (AME) contrast value
3-arm against / neutral / for 2 for vs neutral \(\delta\)
2-arm against / for 3 for vs against \(2\delta\)

\(\delta\) is the per-arm shift (a biased summary’s pull toward its stance). The 2-arm contrast is \(2\delta\) under symmetric arms.

Proposition pool. 12 propositions (selection rule in §3).

Power. 90% power, two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\). The contrast SE is simulated under the model above with the fitted variance components over the 12-proposition pool; it scales as \(SE(N)=se_\text{unit}/\sqrt{N_\text{readers}}\), so \(n=\big((z_{1-\alpha/2}+z_\text{power})\,se_\text{unit}/\text{AME}\big)^2\).

Cost model (Paul’s): £12 per rater-hour (£9 + 33% Prolific fee); 1.5 min per paragraph + 2.5 min fixed overhead per rater. Both designs are 6 paragraphs \(\Rightarrow\) £2.30 per rater.

## 39,980 reader-paragraph rows | 7,996 readers | 98 propositions

2. Variance components and contrast standard errors

The RQ2 model is refit (full validation in Appendix A); its variance components feed the power simulation.

d2 <- df %>% filter(!is.na(stance_shift), !is.na(condition5))
m2 <- glmmTMB(stance_shift ~ condition5 + (1 | reader_id) + (1 | proposition_id),
              data = d2, family = gaussian())
vc <- VarCorr(m2)$cond
varcomp <- list(reader_sd = as.numeric(attr(vc$reader_id, "stddev")),
                prop_sd   = as.numeric(attr(vc$proposition_id, "stddev")),
                resid_sd  = sigma(m2))
tibble(component = c("reader", "proposition", "residual", "marginal"),
       sd = c(varcomp$reader_sd, varcomp$prop_sd, varcomp$resid_sd,
              sqrt(varcomp$reader_sd^2 + varcomp$prop_sd^2 + varcomp$resid_sd^2))) %>%
  kable(digits = 3)
component sd
reader 2.937
proposition 1.924
residual 15.866
marginal 16.250 
set.seed(1)
SE_3 <- simulate_ame_se_unit(varcomp, arms = c("neutral", "for", "against"),
                             qpa = 2, contrast = c("neutral", "for"))$se_unit
SE_2 <- simulate_ame_se_unit(varcomp, arms = c("against", "for"),
                             qpa = 3, contrast = c("against", "for"))$se_unit
tibble(design = c("3-arm (for vs neutral, 2/arm)", "2-arm (for vs against, 3/arm)"),
       se_unit = c(SE_3, SE_2)) %>% kable(digits = 2)
design se_unit
3-arm (for vs neutral, 2/arm) 15.84
2-arm (for vs against, 3/arm) 12.94

The simulated AME SE equals the glmmTMB coefficient SE (identical to \(10^{-6}\)), so the uncertainty driving the power calculation is computed exactly as in Appendix A. Direct check (3-arm): at the N predicted for 90% power at \(\delta = 3\) (N \(\approx\) 293), 200 simulated experiments have the AME 95% CI exclude 0 in 92% of cases.

3. Proposition selection

Rule: the 6 left-coded and 6 right-coded propositions with the lowest mean writer stance-extremity (mean \(|\text{writer\_stance\_pre}-50|\) from the main study), 12 total. Realised persuadability (mean oriented shift in Paul’s follow-up) shown for reference.

feats <- proposition_features(DATA)
y <- persuadability(df)
kable(select_propositions(feats, y, n_per_side = 6), digits = 2)
proposition_id proposition_leaning w_extremity w_conf n_writers persuadability
248 left 21.53 54.56 45 0.91
346 left 22.09 63.02 44 6.51
353 left 22.22 60.38 45 4.80
82 left 22.26 59.57 46 6.67
390 left 22.63 67.93 46 3.31
356 left 22.76 66.22 46 2.39
184 right 16.93 63.76 45 4.54
230 right 18.11 65.76 45 4.62
187 right 18.98 64.33 45 3.99
342 right 20.84 68.11 45 4.75
377 right 22.43 70.84 44 5.84
374 right 22.49 63.84 45 3.80

Two per-proposition metrics, both averaged over Paul’s treated documents:

  • oriented belief shift — movement in the direction the document advocates, \(\text{sign}(\text{perceived\_stance}-50)\times(\text{attitude}_\text{post}-\text{attitude}_\text{pre})\), using the perceived (annotator-labelled) stance for orientation and excluding documents with perceived stance in [40, 60] (no clear advocated direction);
  • agreement belief shift — movement toward agreeing with the proposition, \(\text{attitude}_\text{post}-\text{attitude}_\text{pre}\) (over all treated documents).

Each point is a proposition (coloured by leaning); circled points are the 12 selected by the rule above.

4. Power and cost: 3-arm vs 2-arm

Total cost to detect a per-arm shift \(\delta\) at 90% power (two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\)), for both designs. The 3-arm design detects the for-vs-neutral AME (\(\delta\)); the 2-arm design detects the for-vs-against AME (\(2\delta\)).

designs <- list(
  list(label = "3-arm (for vs neutral)", se_unit = SE_3, mult = 1, questions = 6),
  list(label = "2-arm (for vs against)", se_unit = SE_2, mult = 2, questions = 6)
)
cost_table(deltas = c(1, 2, 5, 10), designs = designs) %>%
  select(design, delta, n_raters, total_cost_gbp) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = design, values_from = c(n_raters, total_cost_gbp)) %>%
  kable()
delta n_raters_3-arm (for vs neutral) n_raters_2-arm (for vs against) total_cost_gbp_3-arm (for vs neutral) total_cost_gbp_2-arm (for vs against)
1 2638 440 6067 1012
2 660 110 1518 253
5 106 18 244 41
10 27 5 62 12

Per-arm shift detectable at each budget (where the curves meet the budget lines):

design budget_gbp min_detectable_delta
2-arm (for vs against) 400 1.59
3-arm (for vs neutral) 400 3.89
2-arm (for vs against) 800 1.12
3-arm (for vs neutral) 800 2.75

5. Paul’s analysis on £400 / £800 of data

Empirical complement to §4: re-run Paul’s exact RQ1 analysis (stance_shift ~ paragraph_type + (1|reader) + (1|proposition)) on random subsamples of his data sized to a £400 and £800 budget. At Paul’s per-rater cost (5 paragraphs = £2.00), £400 buys 200 readers and £800 buys 400 readers. Each budget is resampled 50 times; readers are the sampling unit.

Each treated arm’s mean shift is tested against 0 (its avg_predictions marginal mean, not a contrast against the control arm); the AI-vs-human contrast is also reported.

PAUL_RATER <- cost_per_rater(5)                 # Paul's design: 5 paragraphs/rater
budgets <- c(400, 800)
n_by_budget <- setNames(round(budgets / PAUL_RATER), paste0("£", budgets))
TERMS <- c("AI (vs 0)", "human (vs 0)", "AI vs human")
res <- bind_rows(lapply(seq_along(budgets), function(i)
  rerun_subsample(df, n_readers = n_by_budget[i], reps = 50, seed0 = 1000 * i) %>%
    mutate(budget = paste0("£", budgets[i]), n_readers = n_by_budget[i])))

res %>% group_by(budget, n_readers, term) %>%
  summarise(median_AME = median(est), median_CI_low = median(lo), median_CI_high = median(hi),
            pct_CI_excludes_0 = round(100 * mean(excludes0)), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(match(term, TERMS), n_readers) %>%
  kable(digits = 2)
budget n_readers term median_AME median_CI_low median_CI_high pct_CI_excludes_0
£400 200 AI (vs 0) 4.18 2.52 5.82 100
£800 400 AI (vs 0) 4.29 3.14 5.37 100
£400 200 human (vs 0) 2.84 1.17 4.46 98
£800 400 human (vs 0) 2.78 1.59 3.92 98
£400 200 AI vs human 1.28 -0.89 3.55 20
£800 400 AI vs human 1.61 0.03 3.07 50

pct_CI_excludes_0 is the share of random subsamples in which the 95% Wald CI clears 0 — the realised detection rate at that budget on real data.

6. Secondary analysis: persuasion by document stance

Persuasive (treated) documents are split by their writer-labelled stance — the writer’s own pre-stance from the main study (writer_stance_pre, 0–100): for (> 50) vs against (< 50). The outcome is the raw signed attitude change toward the proposition, \(\text{attitude}_\text{post}-\text{attitude}_\text{pre}\), modelled as raw_shift ~ doc_stance + (1|reader) + (1|proposition); the marginal mean shift of each group is reported with its 95% Wald CI.

pr_self <- read_csv(file.path(DATA, "main_phase_1/proposition_responses.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  transmute(writer_id = as.character(writer_id), proposition_id = as.character(proposition_id),
            writer_stance_pre)
treated <- df %>% filter(!is_control) %>%
  left_join(pr_self, by = c("writer_id", "proposition_id")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(writer_stance_pre), writer_stance_pre != 50) %>%
  mutate(doc_stance = if_else(writer_stance_pre > 50, "for", "against"),
         raw_shift  = pa_post - pa_pre)
ms <- glmmTMB(raw_shift ~ doc_stance + (1 | reader_id) + (1 | proposition_id),
              data = treated, family = gaussian())
counts <- treated %>% count(doc_stance, name = "n_ratings")
avg_predictions(ms, by = "doc_stance", type = "response", re.form = NA) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  transmute(doc_stance, mean_raw_shift = estimate, conf.low, conf.high) %>%
  left_join(counts, by = "doc_stance") %>%
  kable(digits = 2)
doc_stance mean_raw_shift conf.low conf.high n_ratings
against -3.27 -3.97 -2.57 14660
for 4.03 3.32 4.73 14083

A for document moves attitudes toward the proposition (positive raw shift); an against document moves them away (negative). The magnitudes index the per-arm shift \(\delta\) in each direction (the symmetry assumed by the 2-arm design in §4).

Distribution of the writer-labelled stance of the 800 distinct documents Paul showed to persuasion participants:

Granular stance–response (non-linearity)

The for/against split above is coarsened to one cut at 50. Here the document stance enters more granularly, measured two ways separately: the writer-labelled stance (writer_stance_pre) and the annotator-labelled stance (the perceived writer_stance from the main-study annotations). Outcome is the raw signed shift toward the proposition. Each stance is (a) bucketed into deciles, fit as raw_shift ~ bucket + (1|reader) + (1|proposition) (per-decile marginal mean), and (b) entered as a natural cubic spline ns(stance, 4) for a smooth fit.

treated_g <- df %>% filter(!is_control) %>%
  left_join(pr_self, by = c("writer_id", "proposition_id")) %>%
  mutate(raw_shift = pa_post - pa_pre)
sr_writer <- stance_response(treated_g %>% transmute(raw_shift, stance = writer_stance_pre, reader_id, proposition_id))
sr_annot  <- stance_response(treated_g %>% transmute(raw_shift, stance = writer_stance,     reader_id, proposition_id))
buckets <- bind_rows(sr_writer$buckets %>% mutate(source = "writer-labelled"),
                     sr_annot$buckets  %>% mutate(source = "annotator-labelled"))
spline  <- bind_rows(sr_writer$spline %>% mutate(source = "writer-labelled"),
                     sr_annot$spline  %>% mutate(source = "annotator-labelled"))
buckets %>% transmute(source, stance_bucket = bucket, mid, mean_raw_shift = estimate,
                      conf.low, conf.high, n) %>%
  arrange(source, mid) %>% kable(digits = 2)
source stance_bucket mid mean_raw_shift conf.low conf.high n
annotator-labelled [0,10] 5 -5.78 -6.65 -4.91 3225
annotator-labelled (10,20] 15 -5.70 -6.48 -4.92 4503
annotator-labelled (20,30] 25 -3.80 -4.64 -2.95 3435
annotator-labelled (30,40] 35 -3.00 -3.95 -2.06 2147
annotator-labelled (40,50] 45 -1.31 -2.18 -0.44 3045
annotator-labelled (50,60] 55 0.54 -0.42 1.50 2032
annotator-labelled (60,70] 65 3.11 2.12 4.09 1914
annotator-labelled (70,80] 75 3.96 3.02 4.91 2209
annotator-labelled (80,90] 85 5.98 5.20 6.75 4573
annotator-labelled (90,100] 95 6.18 5.41 6.96 4901
writer-labelled [0,10] 5 -5.16 -5.99 -4.33 6191
writer-labelled (10,20] 15 -4.28 -5.32 -3.24 2197
writer-labelled (20,30] 25 -3.97 -5.07 -2.87 1857
writer-labelled (30,40] 35 -1.27 -2.28 -0.27 2442
writer-labelled (40,50] 45 -0.05 -0.88 0.78 5214
writer-labelled (50,60] 55 1.81 0.76 2.86 2104
writer-labelled (60,70] 65 4.12 3.20 5.04 3475
writer-labelled (70,80] 75 5.00 3.93 6.07 1969
writer-labelled (80,90] 85 5.35 4.37 6.34 2704
writer-labelled (90,100] 95 4.98 4.06 5.90 3831

Persuasive impact rises monotonically with document stance through the middle of the scale but flattens (and slightly reverses) in the most extreme deciles under both stance measures.

Appendix A: Validation against Paul’s data

Point estimates, standard errors and 95% CIs for the average marginal effects and condition means are computed with marginaleffects (delta method, re.form = NA) — Paul’s exact procedure — and compared with the values committed in results/followup_persuasion. (His SE is recovered as estimate/z for the AMEs and CI-width\(/2z_{.975}\) for the condition means.)

if (!exists("m1")) {                            # m1 is fit in §5; reuse it
  d1 <- df %>% filter(!is.na(stance_shift), !is.na(paragraph_type3))
  m1 <- glmmTMB(stance_shift ~ paragraph_type3 + (1 | reader_id) + (1 | proposition_id),
                data = d1, family = gaussian())
}
ac_ref  <- avg_comparisons(m1, variables = list(paragraph_type3 = "reference"),
                           type = "response", re.form = NA)
ac_pair <- avg_comparisons(m1, variables = list(paragraph_type3 = c("human", "ai")),
                           type = "response", re.form = NA)
preds   <- avg_predictions(m2, by = "condition5", type = "response", re.form = NA)
quantity estimate se ci_low ci_high paul_est paul_se |d est| |d SE| |d CI|
AME: AI vs control 4.3254 0.2180 3.8980 4.7528 4.3254 0.2180 0 0.0000 0.0000
AME: human vs control 2.9127 0.2180 2.4854 3.3400 2.9127 0.2180 0 0.0000 0.0000
AME: AI vs human 1.4127 0.1780 1.0639 1.7616 1.4127 0.1780 0 0.0000 0.0000
mean shift: control -0.0128 0.2684 -0.5389 0.5133 -0.0128 0.2684 0 0.0000 0.0000
mean shift: human-low 4.4489 0.2709 3.9179 4.9799 4.4489 0.2715 0 0.0006 0.0011
mean shift: human-high 1.3491 0.2708 0.8183 1.8799 1.3491 0.2706 0 0.0003 0.0005
mean shift: ai-low 4.4098 0.2706 3.8794 4.9401 4.4098 0.2722 0 0.0016 0.0032
mean shift: ai-high 4.2148 0.2700 3.6855 4.7440 4.2148 0.2698 0 0.0003 0.0005

Condition means with Paul’s model 95% CIs (avg_predictions):

order mean sd n
